heroOverview: Anisocoria is a difference in the size of the two pupils; a size difference of 0.4 mm or greater is detectable to the naked eye, while up to 20% of the healthy population has a small physiological difference of less than 1 mm that carries no clinical meaning. What matters clinically is whether the inequality is new, whether it changes with light level, and which pupil — the larger or the smaller — is the abnormal one. A new onset of anisocoria with drooping eyelid, neck pain, or headache can signal life-threatening conditions including carotid artery dissection, brain aneurysm, or herniation, and demands immediate neurological evaluation. When anisocoria is long-standing and symmetrically reactive to light, it is almost always benign. Ophthalmologists and neurologists together evaluate ambiguous cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Physiological anisocoria found in up to 20% of the general population; pathological anisocoria is far less common
keyStats.demographics: Affects all age groups; physiological form equally distributed across sexes; pathological causes vary by age and clinical context
keyStats.avgAge: No single peak age; Horner syndrome and aneurysmal third-nerve palsy more common in adults; pharmacological causes seen at any age
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global prevalence not established for pathological anisocoria; it is among the most common pupillary signs encountered in emergency neurology

definition: Anisocoria (ICD-10: H57.02) is the condition in which the two pupils are unequal in diameter. The pupil is regulated by two opposing sets of smooth muscle in the iris: the sphincter pupillae, innervated by the parasympathetic fibres of the third cranial nerve via the ciliary ganglion, and the dilator pupillae, driven by sympathetic fibres that originate in the hypothalamus, descend through the lateral brainstem and cervical cord, and synapse in the superior cervical ganglion before reaching the eye via the nasociliary branch of the first division of the trigeminal nerve. Anisocoria therefore reflects an imbalance in either parasympathetic or sympathetic tone, or can result from structural iris disease, drugs, or eye drops. The clinical imperative is to determine which pupil is abnormal. If the larger pupil fails to constrict in bright light, parasympathetic damage is likely — as in a compressive third-nerve palsy from a posterior communicating artery aneurysm. If the smaller pupil fails to dilate in dim light, sympathetic paresis is likely — as in Horner syndrome from carotid dissection, Pancoast tumour, or cervical cord lesion. Physiological anisocoria, the most common form, shows equal reactivity in both pupils and a consistent difference across lighting conditions.

typesClassification.description: A benign, long-standing difference of up to 1 mm that is equally present in bright and dim light, with both pupils reacting normally. Found in roughly 20% of healthy individuals and requires no investigation once confirmed stable.

Caused by topical or systemic drugs — mydriatic eye drops, atropine, scopolamine patches, or even plant contacts such as belladonna can produce a large, poorly reactive pupil; conversely, pilocarpine and other miotics produce miosis. Usually identified by the pattern and recent medication history.

Sympathetic paresis causing ipsilateral miosis, ptosis, and anhidrosis. The smaller pupil dilates poorly in dim light. Causes range from carotid artery dissection and Pancoast tumour to brainstem stroke.

Compression or ischaemia of the oculomotor nerve. A compressive cause (especially aneurysm of the posterior communicating artery) characteristically dilates the affected pupil because parasympathetic fibres run on the nerve's outer surface; ischaemic third-nerve palsy from diabetes typically spares the pupil.

A benign postganglionic parasympathetic denervation causing a large pupil that reacts sluggishly to light but constricts briskly to near focus and shows supersensitivity to dilute pilocarpine (0.1%). Most common in young women.
Trauma, anterior uveitis, posterior synechiae, or previous intraocular surgery can mechanically distort the iris and produce unequal pupils that do not reflect a neurological lesion.

primarySymptoms: Visible difference in pupil size — one pupil appears noticeably larger or smaller than the other when viewed in a mirror or under normal room light.
Reduced or absent constriction of the affected pupil in bright light (when the larger pupil is abnormal and parasympathetic function is impaired).
Reduced or absent dilation of the affected pupil in dim or dark environments (when the smaller pupil is abnormal and sympathetic function is impaired).
Eyelid drooping (ptosis) on the side of the smaller pupil in Horner syndrome, or variable ptosis with third-nerve palsy.
Double vision or eye movement limitation accompanying third-nerve palsy, where the eye drifts outward and downward.
Light sensitivity (photophobia) in the eye with the larger, poorly reactive pupil, because the iris cannot adequately reduce glare.
Blurred near vision or difficulty reading in Adie's tonic pupil, since near accommodation is also impaired.
Neck or face pain radiating to the jaw or ear on the affected side, a warning feature of carotid artery dissection causing Horner syndrome.
A preceding headache described as the worst of life, suggesting subarachnoid haemorrhage from a ruptured aneurysm causing third-nerve palsy.
Absence of sweating on the same side of the face (anhidrosis) in complete Horner syndrome when the lesion is proximal to the superior cervical ganglion.
earlyWarningSigns: Noticing for the first time that pupils look different in a photograph or mirror when this was not true before.
A slightly drooping upper eyelid on one side appearing alongside any pupil asymmetry.
Mild neck pain or a stiff neck concurrent with a new pupil difference, suggesting possible carotid or vertebral artery involvement.
Gradual blurring of vision or difficulty reading in one eye with a large, poorly responsive pupil.
emergencySigns: A new large pupil that does not constrict to light combined with a headache described as the worst ever experienced — suspect ruptured aneurysm; call emergency services immediately.
Newly unequal pupils after head or neck trauma — possible herniation or carotid dissection; emergency evaluation required.
Large fixed dilated pupil with an eye that cannot look upward, downward, or inward and a drooping eyelid — third-nerve palsy from a compressive aneurysm until proven otherwise.
Rapidly progressive neck and facial pain with Horner syndrome — carotid artery dissection can progress to stroke within hours.
Any sudden-onset anisocoria in a patient with reduced consciousness, even mildly — may represent transtentorial herniation from raised intracranial pressure.

causes.description: An inherent variation in the balance of autonomic tone to the two irides, present from birth or childhood. Both pupils react normally and symmetrically to light; the difference is consistent and does not exceed about 1 mm in most individuals. No treatment is needed.

Interruption of the three-neuron sympathetic pathway at any level — hypothalamus to brainstem (first order), cervical cord to apex of lung (second order), or superior cervical ganglion to orbit (third order) — removes the dilator drive to the iris, producing ipsilateral miosis. Carotid artery dissection, Pancoast tumour, and brainstem infarction are important causes.

The oculomotor nerve carries parasympathetic fibres that constrict the pupil; compression by a posterior communicating artery aneurysm, tentorial herniation, or other mass stretches these outer fibres and abolishes constriction, producing a dilated, fixed pupil. Ischaemic third-nerve palsy from microvascular disease typically spares the pupil.

Viral or immune damage to the ciliary ganglion destroys most parasympathetic neurons, leaving the pupil large and sluggish. Regenerating fibres misroute to the ciliary muscle, creating brisk near but not light constriction. Found in about 0.05% of the population.

Topical or systemic anticholinergic drugs — atropine, scopolamine patches, cyclopentolate eye drops, jimsonweed, or belladonna alkaloids — block the iris sphincter and produce a large, poorly reactive pupil. Pilocarpine, echothiophate, and opioids produce a small pupil. Drug history and pharmacological testing with dilute pilocarpine distinguish these from neurological causes.

Physical damage to the iris sphincter or dilator muscles from blunt trauma, anterior segment surgery, acute or chronic uveitis, or posterior synechiae mechanically prevents normal pupil movement and can produce persistent anisocoria independent of any nerve lesion.

Acute anterior uveitis from any cause — herpes simplex or zoster, sarcoidosis, juvenile idiopathic arthritis — inflames the iris and can produce a constricted, irregular pupil on the affected side due to sphincter spasm or synechiae.

riskFactors.description: Blunt or penetrating trauma to the head, orbit, or neck can damage any level of the pupillomotor pathways or the iris itself, producing acute anisocoria that must be distinguished from intracranial haemorrhage.

Cerebrovascular disease and hypertension increase the risk of brainstem strokes, which can disrupt sympathetic pupillomotor fibres and produce Horner syndrome as part of lateral medullary or Wallenberg syndrome.
Atherosclerosis of the carotid and vertebrobasilar arteries raises the risk of dissection and ischaemic brainstem events; carotid dissection is a leading cause of Horner syndrome in adults under 50.

riskFactors.factor: Aneurysm risk factors (connective tissue disorders, family history)
riskFactors.description: Conditions such as Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, polycystic kidney disease, and a family history of intracranial aneurysm increase the risk of a posterior communicating artery aneurysm, the most dangerous cause of a dilated unreactive pupil.

Diabetic microvascular disease is the commonest cause of ischaemic third-nerve palsy, which typically presents with diplopia and mild ptosis but spares the pupil; it differs fundamentally from the compressive aneurysmal third-nerve palsy that threatens the pupil.

riskFactors.factor: Lung malignancy or apical chest disease
riskFactors.description: Tumours at the lung apex (Pancoast tumour) invade the sympathetic chain at the thoracic outlet and are among the most important second-order neuron causes of Horner syndrome, particularly in older smokers.

Use of anticholinergic eye drops, scopolamine transdermal patches, or accidental exposure to plant alkaloids is a common and reversible cause of a suddenly dilated pupil that mimics a neurological emergency.

affectedDemographics: Young adults aged 20-40, in whom Adie's tonic pupil is most common, particularly in women
Middle-aged adults with cardiovascular risk factors, in whom carotid dissection and ischaemic third-nerve palsy predominate
Older adults with hypertension or diabetes, in whom brainstem strokes and microvascular third-nerve palsies occur
Patients with a history of head or orbital trauma at any age
Individuals with connective tissue disorders at risk for carotid or vertebral dissection or intracranial aneurysm
Any age with pharmacological exposure, including healthcare workers, carers, or gardeners handling anticholinergic plants
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with a careful history: when did the anisocoria appear, is it new or long-standing, and are there associated symptoms such as headache, neck pain, diplopia, or ptosis? Comparing recent photographs of the patient's eyes can confirm whether the difference is truly new. The clinical examination first establishes which pupil is abnormal by testing both pupils in bright and dim light. If the difference is greater in dim light, the smaller pupil is failing to dilate and a sympathetic lesion (Horner syndrome) is suspected; pharmacological testing with 0.5-1% apraclonidine or 4-10% cocaine eye drops confirms sympathetic paresis. If the difference is greater in bright light, the larger pupil is failing to constrict and a parasympathetic lesion — third-nerve palsy or Adie's pupil — is suspected; dilute pilocarpine 0.1% constricts an Adie pupil due to denervation supersensitivity but not a pharmacologically blocked or a third-nerve palsied pupil. The examination of eye movements, lid position, and the rest of the cranial nerve examination is essential: an ophthalmoplegia with a dilated pupil requires immediate brain imaging to exclude a compressive aneurysm. In any new-onset unexplained anisocoria, urgent MRI or CT angiography of the brain and neck vessels is the standard of care. When physiological anisocoria is suspected, no investigations are needed beyond confirmation that reactivity is symmetric. Urine or serum toxicology and medication review help exclude pharmacological causes.

diagnosticTests.test: Pupillary light response testing in bright and dim light
diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes which pupil is abnormal and whether the lesion is sympathetic (small pupil worse in dim light) or parasympathetic (large pupil worse in bright light).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician shines a penlight into each eye in turn in a lit room and then in near darkness, comparing the size difference in each condition; takes about 2 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Pharmacological pupil testing (apraclonidine or cocaine drops, pilocarpine 0.1%)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms Horner syndrome (apraclonidine or cocaine) or distinguishes Adie's tonic pupil from pharmacological blockade (dilute pilocarpine); localises the lesion in Horner syndrome with hydroxamphetamine drops.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: One or two eye drops are instilled and the pupils are re-examined after 30-45 minutes; the test is painless, though drops may briefly sting.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI brain with and without gadolinium, and MR or CT angiography
diagnosticTests.purpose: Rules out posterior communicating artery aneurysm, carotid or vertebral artery dissection, brainstem infarction, or intracranial mass as the cause of new anisocoria.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI takes 30-45 minutes; CT angiography is faster (5-10 minutes) and used first in emergency settings. An intravenous contrast injection is used for angiography.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Examines the iris for structural damage, posterior synechiae, sector palsy, or abnormal vermiform movements that indicate Adie's pupil.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A focused slit of light is directed through the iris while you rest your chin and forehead on the lamp; takes about 10 minutes and is painless.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Looks for Pancoast tumour or apical lung mass when Horner syndrome is confirmed and no other cause is apparent, particularly in smokers.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard chest X-ray takes minutes; CT chest requires about 10 minutes and may use intravenous contrast.

diagnosticTests.test: Blood glucose, HbA1c, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for diabetes (ischaemic third-nerve palsy), vasculitis, and inflammatory conditions that can cause pupillomotor neuropathy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood draw from the arm; results available within hours to a day.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of anisocoria is entirely directed at the underlying cause — the unequal pupils themselves require no local treatment, and addressing them cosmetically without treating the cause would be both ineffective and potentially dangerous. Physiological anisocoria, confirmed by equal reactivity in both eyes across all lighting conditions and a stable lifelong history, needs only reassurance and documentation. Pharmacological anisocoria resolves once the offending drug wears off or is discontinued, typically within 24-72 hours for most topical agents and up to two weeks for long-acting compounds such as atropine. Adie's tonic pupil carries an excellent prognosis; no specific treatment is required beyond reassurance, and if near vision or photosensitivity is significantly impaired, pilocarpine 0.1% drops exploit denervation supersensitivity to reduce the pupil size and improve accommodation. Horner syndrome demands urgent investigation to identify the anatomical level of the lesion: carotid artery dissection is treated with anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy to prevent ischaemic stroke; Pancoast tumour requires multimodal oncological management including surgery, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy; brainstem infarction follows standard acute stroke protocols with antithrombotic therapy and secondary prevention. Third-nerve palsy from a compressive posterior communicating artery aneurysm is a neurosurgical emergency — endovascular coiling or surgical clipping is performed as soon as the patient is stabilised to prevent rupture and subarachnoid haemorrhage. Ischaemic third-nerve palsy from diabetic microvascular disease resolves spontaneously in approximately 75-90% of cases over 3-6 months with optimised glycaemic control, blood pressure management, and statin therapy. Inflammatory and infectious causes — acute anterior uveitis, herpes simplex iridocyclitis, sarcoidosis — are treated with topical corticosteroids, mydriatics to prevent synechiae, and appropriate systemic antiviral or immunosuppressive therapy. Structural iris damage from trauma is managed conservatively unless intraocular pressure rises significantly. Clinicians should document and monitor anisocoria at each visit; any change in degree, reactivity, or associated features may indicate a new or worsening underlying process requiring re-investigation.

medicalTreatments.name: Anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy for carotid dissection
medicalTreatments.description: When Horner syndrome results from carotid artery dissection, anticoagulation with low-molecular-weight heparin followed by warfarin, or antiplatelet therapy with aspirin, is used to prevent thromboembolism and ischaemic stroke. The Carotid Ischemia and Stroke (CADISS) trial found no significant difference between anticoagulants and antiplatelets, so either is acceptable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Stroke risk reduced to approximately 2% with either treatment in dissection managed in the acute phase, compared with an untreated risk of 15-20% in the first weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Glycaemic control for diabetic third-nerve palsy
medicalTreatments.description: Optimising blood glucose and HbA1c in diabetic patients with ischaemic third-nerve palsy hastens microvascular recovery. Statin therapy and blood pressure control are added to reduce further vascular events.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Spontaneous recovery of diplopia and ptosis occurs in approximately 75-90% of ischaemic third-nerve palsies within 3-6 months with metabolic optimisation.

medicalTreatments.name: Pilocarpine 0.1% eye drops for Adie's tonic pupil
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose pilocarpine exploits denervation supersensitivity to constrict the abnormally large Adie pupil, reducing photosensitivity and cosmetic asymmetry. It also modestly improves near accommodation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in producing visible pupil constriction in approximately 80% of confirmed Adie pupils at the 0.1% concentration that leaves normal pupils unaffected.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral or intravenous corticosteroids for inflammatory causes
medicalTreatments.description: Acute anterior uveitis or sarcoidosis-related pupillomotor involvement may respond to corticosteroids; herpes simplex or zoster uveitis also requires topical or systemic antiviral agents alongside anti-inflammatory therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Remission of acute uveitis-associated iris inflammation in approximately 70-80% of cases with appropriate corticosteroid regimens.

medicalTreatments.name: Endovascular coiling or surgical clipping for aneurysmal third-nerve palsy
medicalTreatments.description: A posterior communicating artery aneurysm causing a dilated unreactive pupil with third-nerve palsy is a neurosurgical emergency. Endovascular coiling is preferred at most centres due to lower morbidity; surgical clipping is used when anatomy is unfavourable.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful aneurysm occlusion in approximately 90-95% of cases with modern endovascular techniques; pupil and eye movement recovery occurs in 60-80% over 3-12 months post-treatment.

surgicalOptions.name: Endovascular coiling or surgical clipping of intracranial aneurysm
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for compressive third-nerve palsy from a posterior communicating artery aneurysm. Coiling places platinum coils inside the aneurysm to occlude blood flow; clipping places a titanium clip across the aneurysm neck at open craniotomy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Aneurysm occlusion rate approximately 90-95% with coiling; recurrence requiring retreatment occurs in about 20% of coiled aneurysms over 10 years.

alternativeTreatments.name: Reassurance and observation for physiological anisocoria
alternativeTreatments.description: No active treatment is needed. The patient is educated about the benign nature of the finding and advised to notify a clinician promptly if the degree of asymmetry increases or new symptoms develop.

alternativeTreatments.name: Prism spectacles or ocular occlusion for diplopia during third-nerve recovery
alternativeTreatments.description: During the recovery phase of a third-nerve palsy, temporary prism lenses or occlusion of one eye with a frosted lens or patch can eliminate double vision and allow the patient to function normally while awaiting spontaneous neurological recovery.

whySeeSpecialist: Any new-onset anisocoria should be assessed by an ophthalmologist or neurologist the same day when it is accompanied by headache, neck pain, ptosis, or restricted eye movement. A specialist can differentiate immediately life-threatening causes — ruptured aneurysm, carotid dissection, uncal herniation — from benign ones using pharmacological pupil testing and rapid neuroimaging, and can coordinate urgent neurosurgical or vascular intervention. Stable, long-standing anisocoria without associated features warrants an elective ophthalmology assessment to confirm the benign diagnosis.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a neuro-ophthalmologist or a general ophthalmologist with a subspecialty interest in pupil disorders and afferent/efferent visual pathway disease. When neurological involvement is suspected, a neurovascular or stroke neurologist is essential. Neurosurgical or interventional neuroradiology access is required at the same centre if an aneurysm is a possibility. For chronic Adie's pupil or physiological anisocoria, any comprehensive ophthalmologist is appropriate.
preventionStrategies: Control modifiable vascular risk factors — hypertension, diabetes, smoking, and hyperlipidaemia — to reduce the risk of brainstem stroke and carotid artery disease that cause Horner syndrome and ischaemic third-nerve palsy.
Avoid unnecessary exposure to topical mydriatic agents; if a scopolamine patch is used for motion sickness, wash hands immediately after handling and avoid touching the eyes.
Wear protective eyewear during activities with risk of orbital trauma to prevent structural iris damage.
Regular monitoring of blood glucose and HbA1c in diabetic patients to reduce the risk of microvascular third-nerve palsy.

Seek prompt medical assessment for sudden neck pain, headache, or any new neurological symptom so that carotid dissection or intracranial aneurysm can be identified before a stroke or haemorrhage occurs.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain blood pressure at target levels through diet, regular aerobic exercise, and adherence to antihypertensive medication.
Stop smoking to reduce the risk of atherosclerotic and neoplastic causes of Horner syndrome, particularly Pancoast tumour.
Control blood glucose and maintain a healthy weight to reduce the incidence of diabetic microvascular neuropathy.
Avoid contact sports or high-impact activities without appropriate eye protection if you have had previous iris trauma.
Report any change in the degree of anisocoria or any new accompanying symptom to a clinician promptly rather than waiting for a routine appointment.

recommended: A Mediterranean-style diet rich in vegetables, fish, whole grains, and olive oil to support vascular health and reduce stroke risk
Adequate hydration to maintain blood viscosity and reduce thrombotic risk
Low-sodium foods to support blood pressure control when vascular causes are present
Foods high in omega-3 fatty acids to support anti-inflammatory vascular effects
avoid: High-sodium processed foods that elevate blood pressure
Excess saturated and trans fats that accelerate atherosclerosis
High glycaemic index carbohydrates that destabilise blood glucose in diabetic patients
Excessive alcohol, which can acutely alter blood pressure and vascular tone

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise — 150 minutes a week of brisk walking, cycling, or swimming — is beneficial for vascular health and supports blood pressure and glycaemic control, reducing the risk of vascular causes of anisocoria. Exercise is unrestricted for physiological or Adie's anisocoria. Patients recovering from carotid dissection or aneurysm treatment should follow their vascular or neurosurgical team's guidance on return to activity; strenuous Valsalva-generating activities are typically avoided for several weeks post-procedure.
dailyManagement: Photograph your pupils in consistent lighting monthly to track any change in size difference.
Carry a card noting the presence of known benign anisocoria to avoid unnecessary emergency workups if examined by a new clinician.
Take all prescribed vascular or metabolic medications consistently to reduce the risk of a new neurological event.
Promptly report any new headache, neck pain, or change in pupil reactivity to a clinician rather than attributing it to the known benign condition.

Attend all follow-up ophthalmology or neurology appointments, as some causes — particularly Adie's tonic pupil — may develop additional features over time including loss of deep tendon reflexes (Holmes-Adie syndrome).
prognosis: Prognosis depends entirely on the underlying cause. Physiological anisocoria is a benign lifelong finding with no clinical consequences. Adie's tonic pupil is similarly benign; the cosmetic asymmetry typically persists and the near-light dissociation becomes more marked over years, but vision and health are unaffected. Ischaemic third-nerve palsy from diabetes resolves spontaneously in 75-90% of patients within 3-6 months. Horner syndrome due to carotid dissection that is treated promptly carries a low stroke risk of approximately 2% and most patients recover fully; the ptosis and miosis may persist partially or completely in some cases. Aneurysmal third-nerve palsy has the gravest prognosis if untreated — ruptured posterior communicating artery aneurysms carry a 30-day mortality of approximately 40% — but early neurosurgical or endovascular treatment substantially improves survival and reduces the risk of further haemorrhage to below 1% per year.
recoveryTimeline: Physiological and pharmacological anisocoria require no recovery. Pharmacological dilation from a single mydriatic application resolves within 4-8 hours for short-acting agents and up to 2 weeks for atropine. Ischaemic third-nerve palsy typically begins to improve at 4-8 weeks and resolves fully or nearly fully by 3-6 months. Carotid dissection-related Horner syndrome may improve partially over months but complete resolution of ptosis and miosis occurs in only about 50% of patients. Surgical or endovascular repair of an aneurysm stabilises the patient within hours; recovery of the third-nerve function then follows the same timeline as other third-nerve palsies, 3-6 months.
complications: Ischaemic stroke from carotid artery dissection causing Horner syndrome — the most urgent preventable complication, occurring in up to 15-20% of untreated dissections in the first weeks.
Subarachnoid haemorrhage from rupture of a posterior communicating artery aneurysm causing third-nerve palsy — a life-threatening emergency with 30-40% 30-day mortality.
Permanent diplopia or eyelid ptosis following incomplete recovery from third-nerve palsy or Horner syndrome.
Anxiety and quality-of-life impact from the cosmetic appearance of unequal pupils, particularly in younger patients with Adie's pupil or residual Horner syndrome.
Missed Pancoast tumour — late-stage lung cancer is more difficult to treat; prompt imaging for unexplained Horner syndrome is critical.

supportResources.name: North American Neuro-Ophthalmology Society — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Patient-facing resources from the North American Neuro-Ophthalmology Society covering pupil disorders, Horner syndrome, and third-nerve palsy.

supportResources.name: NINDS — Brain Aneurysm
supportResources.description: National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke overview of intracranial aneurysm, a key cause of pupil-involving third-nerve palsy.

supportResources.name: NHS — Horner Syndrome
supportResources.description: NHS patient information on Horner syndrome, its causes, investigation, and outlook.

sources.title: Kardon RH. Anatomy and physiology of the autonomic nervous system. In: Miller NR, Newman NJ, eds. Walsh & Hoyt's Clinical Neuro-Ophthalmology, 6th ed.
Jacobson DM. Pupil involvement in patients with diabetes-associated oculomotor nerve palsy. Archives of Ophthalmology, 1998
Biousse V, Touboul PJ, D'Anglejan-Chatillon J et al. Ophthalmologic manifestations of internal carotid artery dissection. American Journal of Ophthalmology, 1998
CADISS Trial Investigators. Antiplatelet treatment compared with anticoagulation treatment for cervical artery dissection (CADISS). Lancet Neurology, 2015
Thompson HS, Pilley SF. Unequal pupils. A flow chart for sorting out the anisocorias. Survey of Ophthalmology, 1976

confusedWithConditions.name: Miosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Miosis refers specifically to a single constricted pupil or bilateral small pupils; anisocoria describes the inequality between the two pupils, which may include one miotic and one normal pupil.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mydriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mydriasis is a single dilated or bilaterally dilated pupil; anisocoria describes the size difference between pupils, which may result from one abnormally dilated eye alongside a normal fellow eye.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cataracts
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A dense cataract can appear to alter pupil appearance when viewed through haze, but the pupil itself remains round and normally reactive; anisocoria involves a true size difference in the pupil aperture.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Glaucoma causes optic nerve damage and visual field loss; acute angle-closure glaucoma can produce a mid-dilated, poorly reactive pupil that mimics anisocoria from a neurological cause but is diagnosed by elevated intraocular pressure and a shallow anterior chamber.

relatedConditions.name: Miosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Horner syndrome, a major cause of anisocoria, produces ipsilateral miosis as its cardinal pupillary sign alongside ptosis.

relatedConditions.name: Mydriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Compressive third-nerve palsy and pharmacological blockade produce anisocoria through unilateral mydriasis of the affected pupil.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Acute angle-closure glaucoma produces a fixed, mid-dilated pupil that enters the differential diagnosis of anisocoria from a neurological cause.

relatedConditions.name: Migraine
relatedConditions.relevance: Ophthalmoplegic migraine, now classified as a cranial neuropathy, can cause third-nerve palsy with pupil involvement during attacks, presenting as anisocoria.

relatedConditions.name: Multiple sclerosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Demyelinating lesions in the brainstem or at the ciliary ganglion can disrupt sympathetic or parasympathetic pupillomotor pathways and produce anisocoria.

faqs.question: Is it normal to have slightly different-sized pupils?
faqs.answer: Yes. Up to 20% of healthy people have a small difference in pupil size called physiological anisocoria. The gap is usually less than 1 mm, both pupils react equally to light, and it has no clinical significance. If you have always had slightly unequal pupils and there are no other symptoms, this is almost certainly benign.

faqs.question: When is unequal pupils a medical emergency?
faqs.answer: Seek emergency care immediately if unequal pupils appear suddenly alongside the worst headache of your life, neck pain, a drooping eyelid, double vision, or any change in consciousness. These features can signal a ruptured brain aneurysm or carotid artery tear, both of which are life-threatening.

faqs.question: What causes one pupil to be bigger than the other?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is harmless physiological asymmetry. Other causes include eye drops or medications that dilate one eye, Adie's tonic pupil from nerve damage, Horner syndrome from a sympathetic nerve lesion, and third-nerve palsy from a brain aneurysm or diabetic nerve damage. Which pupil is abnormal — the large or the small one — guides diagnosis.

faqs.question: How do doctors tell which pupil is the abnormal one?
faqs.answer: By testing pupils in bright and dim light. If the size difference is bigger in dim light, the smaller pupil is abnormal (a sympathetic problem). If the difference is bigger in bright light, the larger pupil is abnormal (a parasympathetic problem). Pharmacological eye drops then confirm the type of nerve damage.

faqs.question: Can diabetes cause unequal pupils?
faqs.answer: Yes. Diabetic microvascular disease can damage the oculomotor nerve, causing a third-nerve palsy with double vision and a drooping eyelid. In the diabetic form, the pupil is usually spared or minimally affected, which is a key feature distinguishing it from the pupil-involving third-nerve palsy caused by a brain aneurysm.

faqs.question: What is Adie's tonic pupil?
faqs.answer: Adie's tonic pupil is a large pupil that reacts very slowly to light but constricts promptly to near focus. It results from damage to the ciliary ganglion, often after a viral infection. It is most common in young women, is benign, and does not indicate a life-threatening condition. Low-dose pilocarpine drops can reduce the size difference.

faqs.question: What is Horner syndrome?
faqs.answer: Horner syndrome is a triad of a smaller pupil, drooping upper eyelid, and sometimes reduced sweating on one side of the face, caused by disruption of the sympathetic nerve supply to the eye. Causes range from benign (a neck muscle injury) to serious (carotid artery tear, lung tumour). Any new Horner syndrome needs urgent imaging.

faqs.question: Can eye drops cause unequal pupils?
faqs.answer: Yes, and this is one of the most common causes of a suddenly dilated pupil. Anticholinergic drops used to dilate the eye during an eye examination, or accidental contact with scopolamine motion-sickness patches or certain plants, can block the pupil sphincter for hours to days. The pupil will not constrict with pilocarpine drops, which helps distinguish it from a neurological cause.

faqs.question: Do I need an MRI scan for unequal pupils?
faqs.answer: Not always, but any new, unexplained anisocoria — especially with headache, neck pain, or eye movement problems — warrants urgent brain and vascular imaging. Stable, lifelong unequal pupils with normal reactivity do not require imaging once confirmed by a clinician to be physiological.

faqs.question: Will unequal pupils go away on their own?
faqs.answer: Pharmacological anisocoria from eye drops resolves in hours to a few days. Adie's tonic pupil typically persists long-term but is benign. Ischaemic third-nerve palsy from diabetes usually recovers over 3 to 6 months. Horner syndrome from carotid dissection may partially or fully resolve over months with treatment. The outlook depends entirely on the cause.

faqs.question: Can a brain tumour cause anisocoria?
faqs.answer: Yes, though it is uncommon. A large tumour that raises intracranial pressure enough to push the brainstem downward through the tentorium (herniation) compresses the third cranial nerve, producing a dilated, fixed pupil on the affected side. This is a neurosurgical emergency. Tumours directly compressing a sympathetic pathway can also cause Horner syndrome.

faqs.question: Is anisocoria a sign of a stroke?
faqs.answer: A new anisocoria accompanied by other neurological symptoms can be a sign of brainstem stroke, which disrupts sympathetic pupillomotor fibres and causes Horner syndrome. However, most strokes do not produce anisocoria, and anisocoria alone without other symptoms is rarely stroke. The combination of new anisocoria with facial numbness, vertigo, or difficulty swallowing requires emergency evaluation.

faqs.question: Can anisocoria affect vision?
faqs.answer: Physiological and Adie's anisocoria do not impair visual acuity. A large Adie pupil can cause light sensitivity and mild difficulty reading. Third-nerve palsy causes double vision and a drooping eyelid that can obscure the visual field. Horner syndrome can cause mild ptosis but usually does not reduce visual acuity unless accompanied by other optic pathway disease.

faqs.question: How is anisocoria treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment targets the underlying cause. Pharmacological anisocoria needs no treatment beyond stopping the drug. Adie's pupil may be treated with dilute pilocarpine drops. Horner syndrome from carotid dissection is treated with antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy. An aneurysmal third-nerve palsy requires emergency neurosurgical coiling or clipping. Diabetic third-nerve palsy resolves with blood glucose optimisation.

faqs.question: Can anxiety or stress cause unequal pupils?
faqs.answer: Acute anxiety activates the sympathetic nervous system, which can transiently dilate both pupils, but it does not reliably produce a lasting difference between the two. Persistent anisocoria is not a feature of anxiety or stress. If you notice a new, fixed pupil size difference regardless of your emotional state, see a clinician to rule out a structural cause.

faqs.question: Can children have anisocoria?
faqs.answer: Yes. Physiological anisocoria is as common in children as in adults. Congenital Horner syndrome from birth trauma or a congenital sympathetic defect presents from infancy, often with heterochromia (lighter iris on the affected side). Any newly observed pupil asymmetry in a child, particularly with ptosis or a head tilt, should be assessed by a paediatric ophthalmologist.

faqs.question: What does a neurologist check for when I have unequal pupils?
faqs.answer: A neurologist systematically tests pupil size in light and darkness, eye movements, eyelid position, facial sensation, and swallowing to map the anatomy of the lesion. They order brain and vascular imaging to exclude aneurysm, dissection, stroke, or tumour, and may request chest imaging if Horner syndrome is found and no other cause is evident.

faqs.question: Should I carry medical identification if I have benign anisocoria?
faqs.answer: Many people with known benign or Adie's anisocoria carry a brief card or note documenting it. Emergency clinicians routinely check for pupil asymmetry as a sign of serious neurological injury, so documenting a pre-existing benign difference prevents unnecessary emergency investigations and reduces anxiety for both the patient and the assessing team.

faqs.question: Can glaucoma cause unequal pupils?
faqs.answer: Acute angle-closure glaucoma causes a mid-dilated, poorly reactive pupil in the affected eye alongside severe eye pain, blurred vision, and nausea. This can mimic anisocoria from a neurological cause but is distinguished by markedly elevated intraocular pressure and a shallow anterior chamber on examination. It requires emergency ophthalmological treatment, not neurosurgery.

faqs.question: How long does it take to recover from third-nerve palsy?
faqs.answer: Ischaemic third-nerve palsy from diabetes typically begins improving after 4 to 8 weeks and resolves fully or nearly fully in about 75-90% of patients within 3 to 6 months, with optimised blood glucose control. Recovery after aneurysm repair is similar in timeline but depends on the degree of initial nerve compression.

metaDescription: Anisocoria means unequal pupils and can signal Horner syndrome, third-nerve palsy or benign variation. Learn causes, when it is an emergency, and treatment.
